metadata.title: Why One Quantum Packet Can Slosh Without Spreading
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = coherent state / contained / non-spreading; CRIMSON = free packet / spreading / widening
metadata.note: Exclusions: no coherent-state expansion in |n>, no Poisson photon statistics derivation, no ladder-operator eigenvalue algebra.

beats.narration_text: A free quantum particle always smears out as it moves. But drop one into a harmonic well and a special packet can swing back and forth forever, keeping its exact shape.

card.copy: Why One Quantum Packet Can Slosh Without Spreading

beats.narration_text: Here is a free Gaussian wave packet. Release it and it spreads. The tails grow wider and the peak shrinks. This is not measurement or disturbance. It is built into the Schrödinger equation for a free particle.
Now place the same packet in a parabolic bowl — a harmonic potential. Same initial Gaussian, same Schrödinger equation. But now the packet oscillates back and forth without ever broadening. Why?

card.copy:
Free particle: spreads forever.
In a parabolic well: never spreads.
Why not?

beats.narration_text: A free packet spreads because its Fourier components — high-k faster, low-k slower — fan apart. The harmonic potential applies a restoring force that pulls the components back. The restoring force exactly cancels the fanning. Width stays locked.

The special packet that achieves this is called a coherent state. Its center oscillates at the classical frequency omega. Its width is the minimum allowed by the uncertainty principle. It maintains sigma-x times sigma-p equals h-bar over two at all times.

Every other quantum state in the well changes shape as it evolves: the probability density distorts, sloshes asymmetrically, or develops bumps. Only the coherent state rides the classical orbit while keeping the minimum-uncertainty Gaussian profile.

Think of it as a ball rolling in a bowl. The ball's position corresponds to the center of the wave packet. The bowl walls correspond to the restoring force. A perfect coherent state is the quantum object that behaves most like that classical ball.

Roy Glauber showed in 1963 that the light inside an ideal laser is in a coherent state. The photons aren't classical, but their collective quantum state has exactly this non-spreading, minimum-uncertainty character. That is why laser beams can travel so far without blurring. He received the Nobel Prize in 2005.

The coherent state is not a curiosity. It is the quantum ground truth behind every laser, every optical fiber, and every quantum optics experiment. The harmonic-oscillator algebra isn't a loose analogy for light — it is the physics of light.
A coherent state rides its classical orbit in a harmonic well. Its Gaussian width stays fixed. Free packet: spreads. Coherent state: minimum uncertainty, forever. That is why laser light can travel.

card.copy:
Free packet: spreads.
Coherent state: sigma_x sigma_p = hbar/2, always.
Classical orbit, quantum width locked.
